summary:
The Levant and adjacent regions of the ancient Near East produced some of the earliest known ceramic figurines — small, hand-modeled clay objects depicting humans and animals — from approximately 7500–6500 BCE. These objects predate the widespread use of pottery vessels in the same region, indicating that the technology of firing clay was first applied to symbolic and ritual objects before it was adopted for utilitarian containers. The figurines are found across multiple Pottery Neolithic and late Pre-Pottery Neolithic sites.

The earliest ceramic figurines from the Levant are typically schematic and abstract, depicting female figures with exaggerated features or stylized animal forms. They have been found in domestic contexts as well as in apparent ritual deposits, suggesting they served multiple purposes ranging from personal talismans to communal cult objects. These objects are part of a broader tradition of figurine production in the ancient Near East that would develop greater regional diversity and artistic sophistication in subsequent millennia.
